text: The laparoscopic cholecystectomy begins with the insertion of an 11mm camera port for the telescope and three additional ports: one in the epigastrium and two 5mm ports in the right hypochondrium along the mid-clavicular and mid-axillary lines. The mid-axillary port is used for fundal retraction. The surgeon starts the operation with posterior dissection of the gallbladder using a hook cautery or Maryland forceps, ensuring hemostasis. The dissection focuses on exposing Calot's triangle by identifying the cystic duct and artery, achieving the critical view of safety. Posterior dissection is crucial to confirm no structures extend from the gallbladder bed into the liver.

The surgeon visualizes the gallbladder and Calot's triangle, ensuring no structures extend into the liver. The cystic duct and artery are carefully dissected using a Maryland dissector, achieving hemostasis. Clips are applied to the cystic duct and artery, two proximally and one distally, before they are cut. The gallbladder is then dissected from the liver bed and removed through the epigastric or umbilical port for histopathological examination.
